children.name: Testudo
children.description: The Mediterranean tortoises — the classic domed, high-shelled land tortoises of southern Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East. Testudo species have been kept as pets for centuries and were traded extensively across the ancient world; Roman soldiers carried them as a food source on campaign. All are under significant conservation pressure from habitat loss, collection for the pet trade, and the slow reproductive rate typical of long-lived reptiles.

children.name: Testudo graeca
children.description: The Greek tortoise, also known commonly as the Moorish tortoise and the spur-thighed tortoise, is a species of tortoise in the family Testudinidae. The species is a medium-sized herbivorous testudinid, widely distributed in the Mediterranean region.

children.name: Testudo hermanni
children.description: The Hermann's tortoise is a species of tortoise native to Europe.

children.name: Testudo kleinmanni
children.description: #REDIRECT Kleinmann's tortoise Category:Reptiles described in 1883.

children.name: Agrionemys
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Horsfield's Tortoise — the only tortoise of the Central Asian steppes, named after the naturalist Thomas Horsfield. Unlike its Mediterranean relatives it inhabits semi-arid grasslands and spends much of the year in torpor: active for only two to three months in spring, it spends the rest of the year in deep burrows escaping both summer heat and winter cold. It is the most widely kept tortoise in the world.

children.name: Kinixys
children.description: Hinged-back tortoises of sub-Saharan Africa — unique among tortoises in possessing a rear hinge on the carapace that allows the back of the shell to be lowered to protect the hindlimbs. Several species reach the savanna and open woodland at the southern edge of the Palearctic zone and are among the most ecologically diverse of African tortoises.

children.name: Kinixys erosa
children.description: #REDIRECT Forest hinge-back tortoise Category:Reptiles described in 1812.

children.name: Kinixys nogueyi
children.description: Distributed across West Africa from Senegal to Ghana and into the Congo Basin, this species inhabits moist savanna and forest margins. It is morphologically similar to K. belliana but distinguished by a more rounded carapace and geographic distribution. Heavy exploitation for bushmeat markets and local traditional medicine poses significant conservation concern.

children.name: Kinixys zombensis
children.description: Described from the Zomba Plateau of Malawi, this species ranges through coastal East Africa from southern Somalia to South Africa. It inhabits coastal forest, thicket, and savanna, and is somewhat more arboreal in behaviour than other Kinixys, occasionally climbing low shrubs. It is Under Revised Taxonomy reclassified from K. belliana subspecies and remains poorly studied.

children.name: Geochelone
children.description: Large continental tortoises with a broad distribution across South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa. The Indian Star Tortoise is named for the striking radiating yellow pattern on each scute — a pattern that breaks up the tortoise's outline in dappled grassland light and is among the most beautiful in the family.

children.name: Geochelone elegans
children.description: #REDIRECT Indian star tortoise Category:Reptiles described in 1795.

children.name: Aldabrachelys
children.description: A genus of giant tortoises from the western Indian Ocean, now represented by a single extant species on Aldabra Atoll in the Seychelles. Once distributed across Madagascar and other Indian Ocean islands, the surviving Aldabra giant tortoise is the world's largest land tortoise after the Galápagos species and one of the longest-lived animals on Earth.

children.name: Aldabrachelys gigantea
children.description: The Aldabra giant tortoise, Aldabra tortoise, or simply giant tortoise, is a species of tortoise in the family Testudinidae and genus Aldabrachelys. The species is endemic to the Seychelles, with the nominate subspecies, A. gigantea native to Aldabra atoll.

children.name: Astrochelys
children.description: Two spectacularly patterned Malagasy giant tortoises whose carapace scutes bear bold yellow star-burst radiations on a dark background. Both species are endemic to Madagascar and face extreme pressure from habitat destruction and collection, making them among the most critically threatened tortoises on Earth.

children.name: Centrochelys
children.description: A genus erected for the African spurred tortoise, the largest mainland tortoise on Earth and the third-largest tortoise species overall. Previously placed in Geochelone, it inhabits the arid Sahel belt of sub-Saharan Africa and is characterised by prominent spurred scales on the hindlimbs and a thick, yellowish-brown shell.

children.name: Chelonoidis
children.description: South American and Galápagos tortoises — a diverse genus whose ancestors floated across the Atlantic from Africa to South America in the Oligocene. The genus includes some of the most iconic megafauna of the Western Hemisphere: the enormous Galápagos tortoises and the red- and yellow-footed tortoises of the South American rainforest.

children.name: Chelonoidis hoodensis
children.description: #REDIRECT Hood Island giant tortoise {{Redirect category shell| }}.

children.name: Chelonoidis phantasticus
children.description: #REDIRECT Fernandina Island Galápagos tortoise.

children.name: Chersina
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the angulate tortoise of southern Africa, one of the most common tortoises on the Cape Peninsula and the most abundant tortoise species in the Western Cape of South Africa. Its name derives from the Greek for 'of dry land', reflecting its preference for dry fynbos and Succulent Karoo habitats.

children.name: Chersina angulata
children.description: #REDIRECT Angulate tortoise Category:Reptiles described in 1812.

children.name: Chersobius
children.description: Tiny padloper tortoises of southern Africa — the smallest tortoises in the world. All three species were formerly placed in Homopus and are restricted to the arid and semi-arid regions of South Africa and Namibia. They are specialists of rocky outcrops and dwarf shrubland, and their flattened shells allow them to squeeze into narrow rock crevices.

children.name: Chersobius boulengeri
children.description: {{Speciesbox | image = Karoo Padloper - RSA.jpg | image_caption = Karoo padloper | status = EN | status_system = IUCN3.1 | status_ref = | genus = Chersobius | species = boulengeri | authority = Duerden, 1906 | synonyms = *Homopus boulengeri Duerden, 1906 *Pseudomopus boulengeri — Mertens, L.

children.name: Chersobius signatus
children.description: {{Speciesbox | image = Speckled Padloper - Homopus Signatus 1.jpg | image_caption = | status = EN | status_system = IUCN3.1 | status_ref = | genus = Chersobius | species = signatus | authority = (Gmelin, 1789) | synonyms = *Testudo signata (nomen illegitimum) *Testudo signata *Testudo juvencella *Pseudomopus signatus peersi *Homopus signatus.

children.name: Chersobius solus
children.description: {{Speciesbox | image = Sitzungsberichte (1910) (14595713120).jpg | status = VU | status_system = IUCN2.3 | status_ref = | genus = Chersobius | species = solus | authority = Branch, 2007 | synonyms = *Homopus bergeri *Homopus boulengeri *Homopus solus *Chersobius solus | synonyms_ref = }}.

children.name: Gopherus
children.description: The gopher tortoises of North America — powerful burrowing tortoises of the arid and semi-arid regions of the United States and Mexico, named for their extensive burrow systems that provide critical shelter for hundreds of other species. As ecosystem engineers and keystone species, gopher tortoises are among the most ecologically important reptiles on the continent.

children.name: Homopus
children.description: Small padloper tortoises of southern Africa, formerly encompassing the Chersobius species as well. The remaining Homopus species inhabit rocky succulent scrub in South Africa and southern Namibia, and are among the least studied tortoise genera due to their small size, secretive habits, and restricted distributions.

children.name: Homopus areolatus
children.description: {{Speciesbox | image = Parrot-beaked Tortoise Homopus areolatus CapeTown 8.jpg | status = LC | status_system = IUCN3.1 | status_ref = | genus = Homopus | species = areolatus | authority = (Thunberg, 1787) | range_map = | range_map_caption = | synonyms = * Testudo areolata Thunberg, 1787:180 * Testudo minuta Thunberg 1788:206 (nomen nudum) * Testudo miniata Lacepède 1788:166 (09:6) (nomen rejectum) * Testudo fasciata Daudin 1801:294 (junior homonym) * Testudo africana Hermann 1804:218 * Chersine tetradactyla Merrem 1820:32 * Testudo areolata pallida Gray 1831d:13 | synonyms_ref = }}.

children.name: Homopus femoralis
children.description: Homopus femoralis, commonly known as the greater padloper, is a small tortoise of the genus Homopus, indigenous to the highveld grasslands of South Africa.

children.name: Indotestudo
children.description: Forest tortoises of South and Southeast Asia — medium-sized tortoises of humid tropical and subtropical forests from India to the Indonesian archipelago. They tend to have flatter, more oval shells than the high-domed African and Mediterranean tortoises, and all three species are under severe pressure from hunting and the Asian food and medicine trade.

children.name: Malacochersus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the pancake tortoise — unique among tortoises for its remarkably flat, highly flexible shell. The bones of the carapace are incomplete and paper-thin, allowing the animal to inflate its lungs to wedge itself into rock crevices rather than withdrawing into its shell when threatened.

children.name: Manouria
children.description: The Asian giant tortoises — the two largest tortoises of mainland Asia, inhabiting the upland forests of South and Southeast Asia. Manouria is thought to be the most primitive living genus of tortoises based on molecular phylogenetics, retaining several ancestral skeletal features. Both species are Endangered and declining rapidly due to forest loss and consumption.

children.name: Psammobates
children.description: Three small, patterned tortoises of southern Africa, endemic to the Fynbos and Succulent Karoo biomes of South Africa; collectively known as tent or geometric tortoises. Each scute on their carapaces bears distinct radiating pale lines on a dark background, providing extraordinary camouflage among dry vegetation. All three species have limited distributions and face conservation challenges.

children.name: Pyxis
children.description: Two small, highly distinctive Malagasy tortoises — the spider tortoise and the flat-tailed spider tortoise — endemic to the arid southwestern coast of Madagascar. Both species have a remarkable hinge on the plastron (unusual in non-Kinixys tortoises) that allows females to close the front lobe over the limbs. Both are Critically Endangered.

children.name: Stigmochelys
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the leopard tortoise — the largest tortoise in Africa after the sulcata and one of the most widespread, occurring across sub-Saharan savannas from Sudan and Ethiopia south to the Cape. The leopard tortoise was placed in Geochelone for many years but molecular data confirms it forms its own distinct lineage.
